questions.question: What is an interface in TypeScript?
options: A) A runtime object that validates data
B) A contract that defines the shape of an object at the type level
C) A class that cannot be instantiated
D) A function type declaration

questions.question: Which keyword is used to define an interface?
options: A) type
B) class
C) interface
D) struct

questions.question: What does `readonly` do on an interface property?
options: A) Makes the property optional
B) Prevents the property from being reassigned after initialization
C) Makes the property private
D) Ensures the property is always defined

questions.question: How do you mark a property as optional in an interface?
options: A) name: string | undefined
B) optional name: string
C) name?: string
D) name: string = undefined
A) Creates a copy of the Animal interface
B) Dog inherits all properties from Animal and adds its own
C) Replaces Animal with Dog
D) Creates a union of Animal and Dog

questions.question: Can an interface extend multiple interfaces?
options: A) No, only single inheritance is allowed
B) Yes, using: interface C extends A, B { }
C) Only with the 'implements' keyword
D) Only if the interfaces have no overlapping properties
A) A way to define a fixed set of keys
B) A way to describe objects with dynamic keys where all values share a type
C) A way to access array indices
D) A syntax for defining computed properties

questions.question: What is a method signature in an interface?
options: A) A function implementation inside the interface
B) A declaration of a method's name, parameters, and return type without implementation
C) A static method defined on the interface
D) A constructor signature

questions.question: What happens when two interfaces with the same name are declared in the same scope?
options: A) A compile error occurs
B) The second declaration replaces the first
C) They are automatically merged (declaration merging)
D) Only the first one is used

questions.question: Which of the following is a key difference between `interface` and `type`?
options: A) Types can represent unions and intersections; interfaces cannot represent unions
B) Interfaces cannot have optional properties
C) Types support declaration merging; interfaces do not
D) There are no differences at all
A) No error
B) Runtime error
C) Compile error: Cannot assign to 'id' because it is a read-only property
D) Warning only

questions.question: Which is valid: defining a function type with an interface or a type alias?
options: A) Only type aliases can define function types
B) Only interfaces can define function types
C) Both can define function types
D) Neither can define function types
A) An object that must have a print method that returns nothing meaningful
B) A class called Printable
C) A function named print
D) An abstract class with a print method
A) A compile error because Config is defined twice
B) Declaration merging: the two interfaces combine into one
C) The second Config overwrites the first
D) Only debug is required

questions.question: When should you prefer `interface` over `type`?
options: A) When defining union types
B) When defining object shapes, especially if they might be extended or merged
C) When working with primitives
D) When using intersection types
A) An object where numeric indices map to string values
B) An array of numbers
C) A string-indexed object
D) A map of numbers to numbers
A) Nothing, age is optional
B) Compile error: Property 'age' is missing
C) Runtime error
D) name should be optional

questions.question: Can an interface extend a type alias?
options: A) No, interfaces can only extend other interfaces
B) Yes, an interface can extend a type alias if the type alias describes an object shape
C) Only with a special keyword
D) Only if the type alias is a union
A) Compile error
B) 'Hello, World'
C) undefined
D) 'name'

questions.question: What is structural typing in TypeScript?
options: A) Types are compatible based on their name
B) Types are compatible based on their structure (shape), not their declared name
C) Types must explicitly implement an interface
D) Types must be declared before use
A) No, because obj has an extra property 'age'
B) Yes, because obj satisfies HasName (it has a name: string property)
C) Only with a type assertion
D) Only if HasName also defines age
A) No error; z is ignored
B) Compile error: Object literal may only specify known properties
C) Runtime error
D) z is added to the Point type

questions.question: Which of the following can a type alias do that an interface CANNOT?
options: A) Define object shapes
B) Be extended
C) Represent a union type like string | number
D) Have optional properties
A) A fixed pair of strings
B) A generic interface that works with any two types T and U
C) A tuple type
D) An array of two elements

questions.question: Which feature do interfaces support that type aliases do NOT?
options: A) Optional properties
B) Declaration merging (adding new members in a separate declaration)
C) Readonly properties
D) Generic parameters
